coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-gold hybrid nanoparticles integrated aptasensor for ultra-sensitive detection of vitamin D3 towards point-of-care application
coredata.dc:creator: Wadhwa, Shikha
John, Alishba T.
Nagabooshanam, Shalini
Mathur, Ashish
Narang, Jagriti
coredata.dc:description: Vitamin D is a sunshine vitamin required by the body for various physiological activities. Deficiency of vitamin D (≤29 ng mL−1) can cause dental diseases, sarcopenia, osteoporosis, depression, type 2 diabetes, cancer, etc. Additionally, elevated levels of vitamin D (>150 ng mL−1) can result in numerous infirmities such as anorexia, irregular heartbeat, hypercalcemia, fatigue, etc. Hence, a periodic detection can help maintain an appropriate level (≥30 ng mL−1) of vitamin D in blood serum. Conventional techniques used for the detection of vitamin D are expensive, time consuming, require skilled work force and a specialised laboratory. Herein, we report a portable electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of vitamin D3 using graphene quantum dot-gold (GQD-Au) hybrid nanoparticles. The developed aptasensor has a linear range of 1 nM – 500 nM, limit of detection (LOD) of 0.70 nM (0.28 ng mL−1), limit of quantification (LOQ) of 2.09 nM (0.84 ng mL−1), sensitivity of 0.90 Ω nM−1 mm−2 and a response time < 1 min. The sensor shows high specificity towards vitamin D3, a good stability, shelf life of over 35 days and nearly 98% recovery with serum samples. The developed sensor has been integrated with controlled electronics, thus establishing a portable prototype.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vitamin D3
Electrochemical
Aptasensor
Graphene quantum dot-gold hybrid
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrode characterization at various fabrication steps
Specificity and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Stepwise fabrication of the sensing platform.
(A) FTIR spectrum at different stages of electrode fabrication (B) Nyquist plot at (I) Au-μE (II) Au-μE/MU:MUA/EDC-NHS/GQD-Au (III) Au-μE/MU:MUA/EDC-NHS/GQD-Au/Aptamer electrodes.
(A) Nyquist plot at concentrations 1 nM-500 nM, (B) Calibration plot at 4.4 MHz.
(A) Effect of various interferents on the developed sensor (B) Stability of the electrochemical aptasensing interface for the detection of vitamin D3 (100 nM) over 35 days.
(A) Nyquist plot at concentrations 1 nM-500 nM (B) Calibration plot at 4.4 MHz.
Comparison of the portable prototype with conventional EIS sensing platform inset: portable potentiostat (kStat) circuit.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-gold hybrid nanoparticles integrated aptasensor for ultra-sensitive detection of vitamin D3 towards point-of-care application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Noida 201313, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Noida 201313, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Jamia Hamdard University, Delhi 110062, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, Jamia Hamdard University, Delhi 110062, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vitamin D is a sunshine vitamin required by the body for various physiological activities. Deficiency of vitamin D (≤29 ng mL−1) can cause dental diseases, sarcopenia, osteoporosis, depression, type 2 diabetes, cancer, etc. Additionally, elevated levels of vitamin D (>150 ng mL−1) can result in numerous infirmities such as anorexia, irregular heartbeat, hypercalcemia, fatigue, etc. Hence, a periodic detection can help maintain an appropriate level (≥30 ng mL−1) of vitamin D in blood serum. Conventional techniques used for the detection of vitamin D are expensive, time consuming, require skilled work force and a specialised laboratory. Herein, we report a portable electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of vitamin D3 using graphene quantum dot-gold (GQD-Au) hybrid nanoparticles. The developed aptasensor has a linear range of 1 nM – 500 nM, limit of detection (LOD) of 0.70 nM (0.28 ng mL−1), limit of quantification (LOQ) of 2.09 nM (0.84 ng mL−1), sensitivity of 0.90 Ω nM−1 mm−2 and a response time < 1 min. The sensor shows high specificity towards vitamin D3, a good stability, shelf life of over 35 days and nearly 98% recovery with serum samples. The developed sensor has been integrated with controlled electronics, thus establishing a portable prototype.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin D (sunshine vitamin) is an essential fat-soluble vitamin required for a variety of vital physiological activities. Individuals with deficient levels of vitamin D are growing at an alarming rate, around the globe. Due to numerous environmental and socio-economic factors, people are unable to derive sufficient quantity of vitamin D required for healthy functioning of their bodies. In addition, due to indirect symptoms individuals are unaware to get themselves monitored.

Maintaining optimum levels of vitamin D (≥30 ng mL−1) is essential for staying free from maladies. Deficiency of vitamin D (≤29 ng mL−1) can lead to ocular infection, dental and periodontal diseases, respiratory viral infections [1], sarcopenia [2], osteoporosis [3], type 2 diabetes [4], cancer [5,6], depression, alcoholism and schizophrenia [7,8]. In addition, toxicity of vitamin D3 (>150 ng mL−1) can lead to hypercalcemia [9], constipation, anorexia, muscle pain, joint pain and irregular heartbeat [10].

Vitamin D can be found in different forms however, only vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) are significantly present in the body. Amongst vitamin D2 and D3, the latter has a higher biological activity in maintaining the overall vitamin D status. This is mainly because it has a superior affinity towards vitamin D binding receptors and is a preferred substrate for the hydroxylase enzyme present in the liver (which is responsible for the conversion of vitamin D to 25-hydroxyvitamin D) [11] and has a higher half-life [12].

Some of the conventional techniques utilized for the detection of vitamin D3 include radioimmunoassay [13], chemiluminescence immunoassay [14], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [16]. The disadvantages of such techniques are that these are time-consuming, expensive, use elaborate machinery and require high volumes of patient samples along with skilled lab technicians for analysis. Therefore, it is has become the need of the hour to develop point-of-care (POC) devices that are portable, user friendly and extremely cost effective in order to perform relevant medical interventions to overcome insufficiency, deficiency and toxicity of vitamin D3 in humans.

In contrast to the conventional diagnostic techniques, electrochemical biosensors have been established as an economic, rapid, highly sensitive and simple tool, which facilitates POC monitoring and diagnosis of medical ailments [17]. Previously reported biosensors for the determination of vitamin D3 levels utilize numerous bio-recognition elements such as enzymes [18] and antibodies [19] immobilized onto glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and indium tin oxide (ITO) respectively. Reports have also shown the determination of vitamin D3 using the principle of Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) [20]. However, the drawbacks of such electrochemical biosensors include high cost, instability at room temperature (rapid denaturation) and a shorter shelf life.

The use of Micrux Single Electrodes as sensing platform for smart sensing devices pose a multitude of advantages such as low sample volumes, enhancement in signal-to-noise ratio, etc. [21]. Aptamers are groups of single-stranded nucleic acids that have an ability to specifically bind to a wide range of targets [22] and have been widely utilized as recognition elements. Use of aptamers in biosensors provide several advantages such as they facilitate the generation of signals when an analyte of choice interacts with the aptamer at the electrode surface thereby aiding in the quantification of analytes.

Nanomaterials are found to have unique properties owing to high surface to volume ratio that lead to both physical and chemical differences. Therefore, a wide range of nanomaterials have been employed in the development of biosensors. From the plethora of nanomaterials, carbon and its allotropes (especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs)) have become the state-of-the-art material for electrochemical sensing application due to advantages such as low cost in comparison to metals, facile and green fabrication techniques, easy functionalization, and high electron transport properties [23,24]. Modification of GQDs with noble metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au, Ag, Pt, etc.) allow effective and site-specific conjugation of various bio-recognition elements [25]. GQD-metal hybrid nanoparticles exhibit superior electrochemical, electronic properties and have been extensively used in electrochemical sensors. Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) co-modified glassy carbon electrode have been designed for catechol sensing [26]. A synergistic cooperation between Au nanoparticles and carbon dots is reported to increase specific surface area and enhance electronic and catalytic properties of glassy carbon electrode for sensing applications. A bimetallic nano composite incorporating Au-Pt nanoparticles along with GQDs has been fabricated [27] for simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine (DA), uric Acid and tryptophan. Pd nano particles decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots@N-doped carbon hollow nano spheres demonstrated high electrochemical performance due to the synergistic effect derived from their unique structure and extraordinary electrocatalytic properties [28].

Herein, we report for the first time an electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of vitamin D3 using GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles. The hybrid structure provides suitable functional groups that facilitate faster electron transfer kinetics for electrochemical sensing of vitamin D3 and superior optical and electronic properties. The developed aptasensor has also been integrated with miniaturized electronics for rapid and timely theragnostic.

Micrux Standard Electrodes (Au-μE) (10 × 6 × 0.75 mm3) were purchased from Micrux Technologies (Spain). Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) was purchased from CDH. D-glucose, ethanol and tri-sodium citrate were acquired from Fischer Scientific. Chloroauric acid was obtained from Merck. 1-N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC-HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were procured from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd; while Mercapto-1-undecanol (MU) and 11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (99%) and di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate (99.3%) used for preparing phosphate buffer solution were purchased from SRL. The aptamer sequence was obtained from Eurofins Genomics India Pvt. Ltd. (Bengaluru, Karnataka, India). Serum sample (with known Vit D3 concentration 7.8 ng mL-1) was obtained from diagnostic laboratory and stored at −20 °C. De-ionized water was used throughout the experiments and all chemicals were of research grade.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL JEM F200 microscope), Fourier-transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Frontier Perkin Elmer L1280026), UV–Visible Spectroscopy (UV 1800 Shimadzu) and X-Ray Diffraction (Bruker-D8 Discover with Cu-Kα radiation) were used to characterize GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles. Electrochemical characterization was carried out using Wayne-Kerr 6500B precision impedance analyzer, with a frequency range from 100 Hz to 10 MHz. Portable potentiostat (k-Stat) was purchased from Smokey Mountains, USA.

A 100 µM stock solution of vitamin D3 aptamer (apt-VD3) was prepared using Tris-EDTA buffer (pH 8) and stored at −20 °C for further use. The sequence of the 56-mer aptamer for apt-VD3 was as follows [29]:
5ʹAGCAGCACAGAGGTCATGGGGGGTGTGACTTTGGTGTGCCTATGCGTGCTACGGA A-3ʹ

An aqueous solution of 2.6 µM vitamin D3 was prepared in 100 mL ethanol. Subsequent dilutions of vitamin D3 were prepared in the concentration range of 1 nM − 500 nM in phosphate buffer saline (PBS). 10 µL of each concentration was considered for performing electrochemical analysis. Serum samples (5 µL) were spiked with fixed volume (5 µL) of known concentrations of vitamin D3 in the range 1 nM – 500 nM and were subsequently used for performing impedimetric detection of vitamin D3. For control experiments, serum samples without spiking with vitamin D3 were considered.

Prior to use, Au-μEs were thoroughly cleaned by sonicating them in de-ionized water and subsequently in ethanol for 15 min, respectively. The Au-μEs immobilized with 1:1 vol ratio of 1 mM 11-Mercapto-1-undecanol (MU) and 4 mM 11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA) were left overnight for thiolization and to facilitate the formation of self-assembled monolayer (SAM). The addition of MU:MUA to gold electrodes is an efficient way to control the density of functional groups present on the surface [30]. MU:MUA immobilized Au-μEs were then modified with 1:1 vol ratio of 400 mM N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC-HCl) and 100 mM N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) to activate the carboxylic groups present on the surface. The electrodes were then washed with PBS to remove any unbound molecules. The mixture of EDC-NHS binds with the –COOH groups present on the surface of MUA and forms an active ester group [30]. Onto these SEs, GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles (see supporting information) were immobilized. GQD-Au is bonded through –C = O groups present on GQD and –OH groups of MU. Aptamer was further coated onto the Au-μEs and thus modified electrode was used for the detection of vitamin D3. Immobilization of amine terminated apt-VD3 onto the surface of GQD-Au hybrid is facilitated via amide bond formation. The presence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of GQD-Au aids strong adsorption of aptamer via π-π stacking [31]. The stepwise fabrication is summarized in Fig. 1
.

EIS was performed using a two-electrode setup. The electrolyte used for the study composed of Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) with varying concentration of vitamin D3 ranging from 1 to 500 nM. A bias of 1 V within a frequency range of 100 Hz – 10 MHz was supplied.

Fig. S1 demonstrates characterization of synthesized GQD and GQD-Au hybrid particles using UV–Vis spectroscopy, XRD, FTIR spectroscopy and HR-TEM. Detailed analysis is given in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode characterization at various fabrication steps
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2
A illustrate the FTIR spectra at various stages of electrode fabrication. The peak obtained after immobilization of MU:MUA at 1681 cm−1 corresponds to C = O stretching, while that at 2845 cm−1 and 2918 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching vibrations indicating the formation of self-assembled monolayer (SAM) [32]. After activation of the surface using EDC-NHS linker molecule, the shift in the peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 3384 cm−1 corresponds to –C = O and –COOH or

–NH bond vibrations respectively [33]. An apparent shift in the –C = O peak indicates the formation of an ester bond between MU:MUA and EDC-NHS [34]. The peak obtained at 1716 cm−1 is an evidence of an ester bond formation between EDC-NHS and GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles [35]. After immobilizing aptamer onto the surface, the peak obtained at 3283 cm−1 corresponds to –NH bond vibrations indicating an amide bond linkage between EDC-NHS and aptamer and also with GQD-Au. The peak obtained at 1645 cm−1 corresponding to a bending vibration of –NH, also confirms the amide formation between GQD-Au and the aptamer [36].

EIS was carried out at various stages of working electrode development in the presence of PBS. Nyquist plots at different stages illustrated in Fig. 2B indicate interfacial kinetics of the biosensor. The impedance at various stages is characterized by a semicircle which corresponds to the interfacial charge transfer mechanism (at high frequency) in addition to a linear response that corresponds to the dominance of mass diffusion property over electron transfer process [37]. The semicircle indicates the presence of a parallel double layer capacitance (Cdl) in combination with charge transfer resistance (Rct), in addition to a solution resistance (Rs) [38]. The overall Rct increased upon modifying gold microelectrode with linker molecules (MU:MUA and EDC-NHS) and GQD-Au hybrid particles (Fig. 2B(II) thereafter. The increased resistance upon immobilization of GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles is associated with the electrostatic repulsion between COO– groups present on GQD-Au hybrid particles which act as a barrier layer and hinders the electron transfer from redox probe to the electrode. Moreover, bulky linker molecules cause steric hindrance and inhibit electron transfer process [39]. Despite increased impedimetric response, GQD-Au hybrid nanoparticles are preferred because of their high capability to adsorb biomolecules onto the surface. Subsequent immobilization of amine terminated apt-VD3 (Fig. 2B (III)) via amide bond formation causes further decrease in Rct. The decreased Rct value is indicative of effective immobilization due to reduction in electrostatic repulsion between COO– groups and the redox probe [39]. Since lower Rct value corresponds to higher electron transfer rate at the interface [40]; it is therefore suitable for impedimetric detection of vitamin D3.

The Nyquist plots of various concentration of aptamer is shown in Fig. S2
A. It can be observed that with an increase in aptamer concentration from 10 to 60 µM, the value of Rct decreased. Lowest Rct value was obtained at 60 µM aptamer concentration. Upon further increase in aptamer concentration, the impedance drastically increased due to the presence of a thick organic layer at the surface that causes steric hindrance and subsequently decreases the electron transfer process [41]. Wang et al. observed a similar trend for the electrochemical aptasensing of Malachite Green [42]. 60 µM was hence chosen as the optimum aptamer concentration for further experiments. Fig. S2
B represents the calibration plot at 4.4 MHz (the frequency at which impedance saturates).

Quantitative analysis of vitamin D3 was carried out using EIS spectrum under optimum experimental conditions (see supporting information) in order to validate the developed sensor. Fig. 3
A demonstrates the change in Nyquist plot with an increase in the concentration of the analyte (the impedance data obtained from serum samples were subtracted from the analyte data). It can be observed that when the analyte selectively binds with the aptamer, the resistance increases. This can be attributed to the fact that an increase in the analyte concentration results in target induced folding of the aptamer, consequently changing its structure and forming a complex of aptamer and vitamin D3, which closely attaches itself to the surface of the electrode. Due to the non– conductive nature of the complex formed, the electron transfer process on the surface of the electrode is hindered, thereby increasing the resistance.

The GQD-Au hybrid based aptasensor was calibrated at 4.4 MHz and exhibited a linear range from 1 nM to 500 nM as illustrated in Fig. 3 B. The LoD for the fabricated electrochemical aptasensor was calculated to be 0.70 nM (0.28 ng mL−1) using the 3σ rule, while the LoQ was calculated as 2.09 nM (0.84 ng mL−1) using the 10 σ rule [43]. The sensitivity was calculated to be 0.90 Ω nM−1 mm−2. The fabricated sensor had a response time < 1 min. Table S.1 represents a comparison of performance between the developed impedimetric aptasensor with the reported sensors for the detection of vitamin D3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the specificity of the fabricated sensing platform, different analytes selected were oxalic acid (1 mM), urea (2 mM), uric acid (0.5 mM) and vitamin E (30 mM). The concentrations of the interfering molecules were chosen according to their physiological range in the human blood. Fig. 4
A shows the plot obtained from different interfering molecules and their corresponding value of impedance. From Fig. 4A, it can be observed that the value of impedance for vitamin D3 (at 100 nM) is in the range of 1400–1600 Ω while individual interfering molecules show impedance > 2400 Ω. In addition, when the interfering metabolites were mixed with vitamin D3, the sensor showed similar impedance as that of Vitamin D3. Human serum sample spiked with 100 nM vitamin D3 also yields a similar response as obtained for vitamin D3 alone. This validates that the developed vitamin D3 aptasensor is highly specific to the analyte of interest.

The stability of the developed aptasensing interface was evaluated by monitoring the impedimetric response at a regular gap of 5 days (Fig. 4 B) for over 35 days. Fig. 4B indicates the stability of the developed electrochemical aptasensor over 35 days, however the response is not decaying even after 35 days, thereby confirming its shelf life and reproducibility. For commercial development, it is suggested that the storage conditions of the aptasensor can be optimized which may help in increasing the shelf-life of the sensor further.

In order to check the feasibility and potential application of the fabricated sensor, serum samples were spiked with increasing concentration of vitamin D3 solutions. As observed from the Nyquist plot in Fig. 5
A, with an increase in concentration, the value of Rct increases, exhibiting a similar trend as that for non-spiked samples. This validates the analysis of serum samples. Fig. 5 B illustrates the calibration plot at 4.4 MHz. The LoD, LoQ and sensitivity obtained for spiked serum samples were 2.18 nM (0.87 ng mL−1), 6.62 nM (2.64 ng mL−1) and 0.95 Ω nM−1 mm−2 respectively.

Table S.2 (supporting information) represents the percentage recovery obtained for the detection of vitamin D3 in serum samples. From Table S2, the recovery lies in the range of 98–100%, thereby, demonstrating high sensitivity and selectivity of the developed sensor.

The developed sensor was also interfaced with controlled electronics for development of a portable prototype. It was found that the portable sensor was able to satisfactorily detect the analyte (vitamin D3) (Fig. 6
) and was able to transmit the data wirelessly to a remote device via bluetooth interface. Hence, the prototype can be a suitable replacement for the existing technologies as it can be further developed for POC detection towards connected health applications.

In this work, novel graphene quantum dot-gold (GQD-Au) hybrid nanoparticles were used as sensing platform for developing a state-of-the-art electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of vitamin D3. The sensor gives a linear response in the concentration range of 1 – 500 nM, with LoD of 0.70 nM (0.28 ng mL−1), LoQ of 2.09 nM (0.84 ng mL−1) and sensitivity of 0.90 Ω nM−1 mm−2. The fabricated sensor shows excellent specificity against interfering metabolites as well as stability for over a period of 35 days. The sensor gives reasonable response with serum samples and has also been integrated with controlled electronics, thus establishing a portable prototype for connected health application.

Shikha Wadhwa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Alishba T. John: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shalini Nagabooshanam: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Ashish Mathur: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jagriti Narang: Validation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors AM and SW would like to acknowledge Department of Science and Technology - UK-India Education and Research Initiative (DST-UKIERI) grant (DST/INT/UK/P-131/2016) and Indian National Science Academy (INSA) fellowship (INSA/SP/VSP- 52/2019-20) (AM) for financial assistance. Authors further acknowledge Inter University Accelerator Centre (IUAC), Delhi for HR-TEM facility and Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University Uttar Pradesh for equipment support and laboratory space.
Authors thank all the scientists referenced throughout this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.146427.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
